lines.text: Introduction, part two. The game theory approach.
Most of what poker players know about strategy was learned through an exploitative framework — and most players who use this framework do not even realize that is what they are doing.
Exploitative play means that your strategy depends on assumptions about how your opponents will behave.
Those assumptions might be conscious or unconscious, but they are always there, quietly driving your decisions.
Take one of the most widely known concepts in poker: the continuation bet.
The conventional wisdom is that if you raised before the flop, you should generally make a bet on the flop even if that card did nothing for your hand.
But this is not some iron law of poker handed down from on high.

The profitability of that continuation bet depends entirely on specific assumptions about your opponents — that they tend to call too loosely before the flop, that they give up too easily after the flop, and that they rarely raise as a bluff.
In many games, especially at lower stakes, those are perfectly safe assumptions, and the continuation bet works beautifully.
The danger of exploitative play is the other side of that coin.

Any time you adjust your own strategy to take advantage of a mistake you expect your opponent to make, you are simultaneously opening yourself up to exploitation if that assumption turns out to be wrong.
Your opponents may be actively studying you, trying to figure out your tendencies, and then deliberately doing the opposite of what you expect.
A simple example makes this concrete.
Suppose you decide to bluff at a very high frequency because you believe your opponent folds too often.
If you are right, that is an excellent strategy — you profit handsomely every time he incorrectly releases a hand.
But if you are wrong, and your opponent actually calls too much, then you are the one making an expensive mistake, not him.
Game theory offers a fundamentally different framework for approaching these decisions.
Rather than assuming your opponent will make a particular mistake, the game theory framework assumes that whenever there is a mathematically correct play available to your opponent, he will make it.
Your goal, then, is not to bet on a specific mistake your opponent will make but to create situations where no clearly correct play exists for him.
You want to put him in a position where calling is not obviously right and folding is not obviously right either.
If he calls when you bet and you have a strong hand, he loses.
If he calls when you bet and you are bluffing, you lose.
But if you have constructed your betting range properly — with the right mixture of strong hands and bluffs — then neither calling nor folding will be consistently profitable for him.
He will be stuck, unable to take advantage of you no matter what he chooses.
At this point in the explanation, Brokos anticipates a natural objection.

You are probably thinking about a specific opponent right now — maybe someone who always calls, or someone who always folds — and wondering why you would ever need game theory when you can just exploit that person directly.
And that is a completely valid point.
Game theory is a tool, not a mandate.
If you genuinely believe you know how a specific opponent will behave in a specific situation, by all means build your strategy around that belief.
You do not need game theory in every hand.
But the reality is that you probably do not know how every opponent will play in every situation.
When you run out of reliable reads, or when you are up against a strong opponent whose strategy is hard to pin down, that is when game theory becomes invaluable.
One important clarification about what the game theory framework actually assumes: it does not assume your opponent knows what cards you hold.
What it does assume is that your opponent knows your strategy — meaning he knows the full range of hands you might have in any given situation.
Consider a river all-in bet.
Your opponent does not know that you specifically have a flush, but if you only ever go all-in on the river when you have a flush, he knows that too.
And the counter-strategy is trivially simple: fold everything that cannot beat a flush, call with everything that can.
To prevent your opponent from exploiting you this easily, you need to balance that all-in bet by including some bluffs in your range.
When you sometimes go all-in as a bluff on that river, suddenly your opponent faces a genuine dilemma — calling might be wrong, and folding might be wrong.
He cannot be sure.
This principle of balance applies far beyond river bets.

When you raise before the flop, ideally your range should include some hands that are happy to face a re-raise, some hands that will reluctantly call a re-raise, and some hands that will fold to a re-raise.
If you get that mixture right, your opponents can never reliably predict how you will respond to pressure, which makes the decision of whether to re-raise you a genuinely difficult one for them.
A common misunderstanding is that embracing game theory means throwing all your reads out the window and pretending your opponents always play perfectly.
That is not what game theory asks of you.
Game theory does not require anything of you — it is simply a toolkit, and you get to decide when to reach for it.
When you have a confident read on an opponent and a clear exploitative line to take, take it.

When you are uncomfortable making that kind of assumption — either because your opponent is skilled and unpredictable, or simply because you are in unfamiliar territory — that is when you open the game theory toolbox.
The game theory approach can also open your eyes to exploitation opportunities you would never have spotted otherwise.
There are many situations in poker where it is not at all obvious which hands would make good bluffs, and as a result, most players simply do not bluff in those spots.
But here is the beautiful irony: precisely because bluffing is so rare in those situations, many opponents fold far too often when they do encounter a bet there.

If you can identify these non-intuitive bluffing spots and figure out which hands are the best candidates, you can extract a lot of value from opponents who are over-folding without even realizing it.
Game theory is what helps you find those spots.

The approach in this book is to begin with equilibrium solutions — that is, the strategies where neither player can improve their results by changing what they are doing, assuming the other player plays optimally.

From those equilibrium solutions, you will then learn how to derive exploitative strategies, identifying the specific kinds of mistakes an opponent might make and the best ways to profit from each one.

By the end, you should feel genuinely comfortable thinking in both modes — equilibrium thinking and exploitative thinking — and confident in your ability to decide which mode fits any given situation you face at the table.
